main_paper.raw: {"methods":["GAPSI algorithm (feature-enhanced base-stock policy with AdaGrad learning rates and carefully chosen partial derivatives)","Modeling inventory problems within the Online Policy Selection (OPS) framework","Incorporation of domain-specific features (e.g., seasonality indicators) into policy learning","Comparison with Model Predictive Control (MPC) as a baseline"],"problems":["Online inventory control with partial historical information","Handling non-differentiability in inventory optimization","Managing multiple products, lost sales, perishability, warehouse-capacity constraints, and lead times in inventory systems","Learning effective replenishment policies under non-stationary and regime-changing demand"],"datasets":["Real-world inventory data (details not specified in the provided text)"],"metrics":["Cost minimization (total cost)","Performance relative to classical approaches (e.g., regret, though not explicitly named in the text)"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes GAPSI, a new algorithm for online inventory problems that handles non-differentiability via AdaGrad-style learning rates and carefully chosen derivatives.","Introduces a feature-enhanced base-stock policy that learns target inventory levels as linear functions of demand features or forecasts.","Demonstrates that the OPS framework can model realistic inventory problems with complex constraints (perishability, lead times, capacity, etc.).","Shows that online learning approaches, specifically the proposed method, outperform classical approaches in non-stationary and regime-changing demand scenarios.","Highlights the flexibility of incorporating domain-specific knowledge (e.g., seasonality) into the online policy learning process."]}

methods: GAPSI algorithm (feature-enhanced base-stock policy with AdaGrad learning rates and carefully chosen partial derivatives)
Modeling inventory problems within the Online Policy Selection (OPS) framework
Incorporation of domain-specific features (e.g., seasonality indicators) into policy learning
Comparison with Model Predictive Control (MPC) as a baseline
problems: Online inventory control with partial historical information
Handling non-differentiability in inventory optimization
Managing multiple products, lost sales, perishability, warehouse-capacity constraints, and lead times in inventory systems
Learning effective replenishment policies under non-stationary and regime-changing demand
datasets: Real-world inventory data (details not specified in the provided text)
metrics: Performance relative to classical approaches (e.g., regret, though not explicitly named in the text)
novelty_claims: Proposes GAPSI, a new algorithm for online inventory problems that handles non-differentiability via AdaGrad-style learning rates and carefully chosen derivatives.
Introduces a feature-enhanced base-stock policy that learns target inventory levels as linear functions of demand features or forecasts.
Demonstrates that the OPS framework can model realistic inventory problems with complex constraints (perishability, lead times, capacity, etc.).
Shows that online learning approaches, specifically the proposed method, outperform classical approaches in non-stationary and regime-changing demand scenarios.
Highlights the flexibility of incorporating domain-specific knowledge (e.g., seasonality) into the online policy learning process.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["MaxCOSD algorithm (a generalization of Online Subgradient Descent)","Online Inventory Optimization (OIO) framework (extension of Online Convex Optimization)"],"problems":["Online multi-product inventory control with general (non-i.i.d.) demand processes","Minimizing cumulative losses (regret) in inventory management with unknown, possibly correlated and nonstationary demands","Inventory optimization with general losses and stateful dynamics (including perishability)"],"datasets":["Synthetic data","Real-world data (not specified in detail)"],"metrics":["Regret (cumulative loss compared to optimal policy)"],"results":[{"metric":"Regret (theoretical bound)","value":"O(√T) (both in expectation and in high probability)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes MaxCOSD, an online algorithm with provable guarantees for inventory problems with non-i.i.d. demands and stateful dynamics.","Introduces the Online Inventory Optimization (OIO) framework, extending Online Convex Optimization to inventory problems.","Provides theoretical regret guarantees under minimal (non-degeneracy) assumptions, not requiring i.i.d. demand.","Demonstrates that the non-degeneracy assumption is necessary and sharp for learning in this setting.","Empirically validates the algorithm on both synthetic and real-world data, showing versatility and performance."]}

methods: MaxCOSD algorithm (a generalization of Online Subgradient Descent)
Online Inventory Optimization (OIO) framework (extension of Online Convex Optimization)
problems: Online multi-product inventory control with general (non-i.i.d.) demand processes

datasets: Synthetic data
Real-world data (not specified in detail)
metrics: Regret (cumulative loss compared to optimal policy)

results.value: O(√T) (both in expectation and in high probability)

novelty_claims: Proposes MaxCOSD, an online algorithm with provable guarantees for inventory problems with non-i.i.d. demands and stateful dynamics.
Introduces the Online Inventory Optimization (OIO) framework, extending Online Convex Optimization to inventory problems.
Provides theoretical regret guarantees under minimal (non-degeneracy) assumptions, not requiring i.i.d. demand.
Demonstrates that the non-degeneracy assumption is necessary and sharp for learning in this setting.
Empirically validates the algorithm on both synthetic and real-world data, showing versatility and performance.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["HQL (Hierarchical Q-Learning)","FQL (Flat Q-Learning)","Meta-HQL (Bandit learning algorithm on top of RL algorithms)","Mimic-QL (Policy for nondiscarding multiproduct backlogging model)"],"problems":["Online inventory management with unknown cyclic demand distributions","Lost-sales model with zero lead time and cyclic demand","Multiproduct backlogging model with positive lead times, fixed joint-ordering costs, and order limits under cyclic demand","Reducing regret in inventory models with large state-action spaces"],"datasets":["Real sales data set from Rossmann (large European drugstore chain)","Synthetic data set"],"metrics":["Regret (difference between total expected cost of policy and clairvoyant optimal policy)"],"results":[{"metric":"Regret (episodic lost-sales model)","value":"O(√T)"},{"metric":"Regret (episodic multiproduct backlogging model)","value":"O(√T)"},{"metric":"Regret (nondiscarding lost-sales model with zero lead time, Meta-HQL)","value":"O(√T)"},{"metric":"Regret (nondiscarding multiproduct backlogging model, Mimic-QL)","value":"O(√T)"},{"metric":"Empirical performance (real and synthetic data)","value":"Rapid convergence to optimal policy and dramatic outperformance over i.i.d. demand modeling policy"}],"novelty_claims":["First RL algorithms for online inventory models with unknown cyclic demands that achieve optimal regret bounds matching lower bounds.","Introduction of episodic and nondiscarding models for both lost-sales and multiproduct backlogging settings with cyclic demand.","Development of Meta-HQL, a bandit learning algorithm governing multiple RL algorithms for nondiscarding models.","Regret bounds remove dependence on the cardinality of the state-action space, improving over existing RL algorithms.","Empirical demonstration of rapid convergence and significant improvement over policies assuming i.i.d. demand."]}

novelty_claims: First RL algorithms for online inventory models with unknown cyclic demands that achieve optimal regret bounds matching lower bounds.
Introduction of episodic and nondiscarding models for both lost-sales and multiproduct backlogging settings with cyclic demand.
Development of Meta-HQL, a bandit learning algorithm governing multiple RL algorithms for nondiscarding models.
Regret bounds remove dependence on the cardinality of the state-action space, improving over existing RL algorithms.
Empirical demonstration of rapid convergence and significant improvement over policies assuming i.i.d. demand.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Application of VC theory (Pseudo-dimension and Fat-shattering dimension) to analyze generalization error of inventory policies","Learning inventory policies from data using supervised learning and hindsight reinforcement learning approaches","Restricting policy classes to well-established inventory policies (base-stock and (s, S) policies)"],"problems":["Providing theoretical generalization guarantees for learning inventory policies from data","Quantifying and bounding the generalization (estimation) error for learned inventory policies, especially under arbitrary (possibly correlated) demand sequences","Understanding the impact of policy class complexity on overfitting and generalization in inventory management"],"datasets":["Numerical simulations (synthetic demand sequences)"],"metrics":["Generalization error (difference between empirical and expected performance)","Estimation error"],"results":[{"metric":"Generalization error of T-parameter base-stock policy class","value":"An order of magnitude lower than that of the two-parameter (s, S) policy class (as shown in simulations)"}],"novelty_claims":["First application of VC theory (including Pseudo-dimension and Fat-shattering dimension) to provide generalization guarantees for learning inventory policies, including base-stock and (s, S) policies.","Analysis does not require the common independence-over-time assumption for demand, allowing for arbitrary demand correlations.","Demonstrates that the number of parameters is not a reliable indicator of overfitting/generalization error in inventory policy classes.","Provides theoretical and empirical evidence that simpler policy classes can generalize better, supporting a 'learning less is more' principle in inventory management."]}

methods: Application of VC theory (Pseudo-dimension and Fat-shattering dimension) to analyze generalization error of inventory policies
Learning inventory policies from data using supervised learning and hindsight reinforcement learning approaches
Restricting policy classes to well-established inventory policies (base-stock and (s, S) policies)
problems: Providing theoretical generalization guarantees for learning inventory policies from data
Quantifying and bounding the generalization (estimation) error for learned inventory policies, especially under arbitrary (possibly correlated) demand sequences
Understanding the impact of policy class complexity on overfitting and generalization in inventory management
datasets: Numerical simulations (synthetic demand sequences)
metrics: Generalization error (difference between empirical and expected performance)

results.metric: Generalization error of T-parameter base-stock policy class
results.value: An order of magnitude lower than that of the two-parameter (s, S) policy class (as shown in simulations)

novelty_claims: First application of VC theory (including Pseudo-dimension and Fat-shattering dimension) to provide generalization guarantees for learning inventory policies, including base-stock and (s, S) policies.
Analysis does not require the common independence-over-time assumption for demand, allowing for arbitrary demand correlations.
Demonstrates that the number of parameters is not a reliable indicator of overfitting/generalization error in inventory policy classes.
Provides theoretical and empirical evidence that simpler policy classes can generalize better, supporting a 'learning less is more' principle in inventory management.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Scenario Predict-then-Optimize (ScenPO) approach","Multi-Horizon Quantile Recurrent Neural Network (MQRNN) for scenario prediction","Scenario sampling based on predicted quantiles","Stochastic programming for scenario-based optimization (single-stage and two-stage formulations)","Tailored matheuristic algorithms for solving stochastic programming problems","Progressive hedging for computational tractability","Long Short-Term Memory Neural Networks (LSTM) as alternative scenario-predict step","Empirical sampling approaches","Maximum likelihood distribution fitting for scenario generation"],"problems":["Joint real-time optimization of inventory replenishment and vehicle routing in one-warehouse, multiple-retailer systems under stochastic, nonstationary, and autocorrelated demand","Integrating demand prediction and optimization for online inventory routing without relying on distributional assumptions or single-point forecasts","Improving service levels and truck fill rates in supply chain operations"],"datasets":["Synthetic data with controlled demand nonstationarity","Large-scale real-life data from SAIC Volkswagen Automotive Co., Ltd. (China, 2020)"],"metrics":["Gap reduction between oracle solver (perfect demand knowledge) and expected value solution (cost gap reduction)","Expected total cost (inventory + transportation)","Service level at each retailer","Truck fill rate"],"results":[{"metric":"Gap reduction (oracle vs. expected value solution) - ScenPO (MQRNN + SS formulation)","value":"61.60% average reduction"},{"metric":"Gap reduction (oracle vs. expected value solution) - best classic prediction-focused learning","value":"30.20% average reduction"},{"metric":"Gap reduction (oracle vs. expected value solution) - sample average approximation (Kannan et al. 2022)","value":"51.00% average reduction"},{"metric":"Gap reduction (oracle vs. expected value solution) - standard quantile regression","value":"55.60% average reduction"}],"novelty_claims":["First fully integrated prediction and optimization approach for data-driven online inventory routing that does not make distributional assumptions nor rely on single-point forecasts.","Introduction of a scenario-predict step using MQRNN to generate robust, distribution-free demand scenarios for each retailer.","Development of two distinct stochastic programming formulations (single-stage and two-stage) for scenario-based optimization, with tailored matheuristics and progressive hedging.","Demonstration that quantile-based scenario generation within ScenPO is more robust and effective than residuals-based scenario generation, especially for complex, real-life demand patterns.","Extensive empirical evaluation on both synthetic and large-scale real-life datasets, showing significant outperformance over state-of-the-art benchmarks including decision-focused learning (CombOptNet) and residuals-based sample average approximation.","ScenPO is generic, fast, does not require distributional assumptions, and is easily extendable to other operational constraints."]}

methods: Scenario Predict-then-Optimize (ScenPO) approach
Multi-Horizon Quantile Recurrent Neural Network (MQRNN) for scenario prediction
Scenario sampling based on predicted quantiles
Stochastic programming for scenario-based optimization (single-stage and two-stage formulations)
Tailored matheuristic algorithms for solving stochastic programming problems
Progressive hedging for computational tractability
Long Short-Term Memory Neural Networks (LSTM) as alternative scenario-predict step
Empirical sampling approaches
Maximum likelihood distribution fitting for scenario generation
problems: Joint real-time optimization of inventory replenishment and vehicle routing in one-warehouse, multiple-retailer systems under stochastic, nonstationary, and autocorrelated demand
Integrating demand prediction and optimization for online inventory routing without relying on distributional assumptions or single-point forecasts
Improving service levels and truck fill rates in supply chain operations
datasets: Synthetic data with controlled demand nonstationarity
Large-scale real-life data from SAIC Volkswagen Automotive Co., Ltd. (China, 2020)
metrics: Gap reduction between oracle solver (perfect demand knowledge) and expected value solution (cost gap reduction)

results.metric: Gap reduction (oracle vs. expected value solution) - ScenPO (MQRNN + SS formulation)
results.value: 61.60% average reduction

results.metric: Gap reduction (oracle vs. expected value solution) - best classic prediction-focused learning
results.value: 30.20% average reduction

results.metric: Gap reduction (oracle vs. expected value solution) - sample average approximation (Kannan et al. 2022)
results.value: 51.00% average reduction

results.metric: Gap reduction (oracle vs. expected value solution) - standard quantile regression
results.value: 55.60% average reduction

novelty_claims: First fully integrated prediction and optimization approach for data-driven online inventory routing that does not make distributional assumptions nor rely on single-point forecasts.
Introduction of a scenario-predict step using MQRNN to generate robust, distribution-free demand scenarios for each retailer.
Development of two distinct stochastic programming formulations (single-stage and two-stage) for scenario-based optimization, with tailored matheuristics and progressive hedging.
Demonstration that quantile-based scenario generation within ScenPO is more robust and effective than residuals-based scenario generation, especially for complex, real-life demand patterns.

Extensive empirical evaluation on both synthetic and large-scale real-life datasets, showing significant outperformance over state-of-the-art benchmarks including decision-focused learning (CombOptNet) and residuals-based sample average approximation.
ScenPO is generic, fast, does not require distributional assumptions, and is easily extendable to other operational constraints.

Develop a general paid-review strategy to (partially) decouple current profit generation from future demand induction via online reviews.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Gradient-based Adaptive Policy Selection (GAPS) algorithm","General analytical framework for online policy selection via online optimization","Contractive perturbation property for policy-induced closed-loop dynamics"],"problems":["Online adaptive policy selection in time-varying discrete-time dynamical systems","Minimizing total stage costs in systems with time-varying costs and dynamics","Achieving optimal regret bounds for online policy selection, both in convex and nonconvex settings"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Policy regret","Local regret","Adaptation speed (qualitative, in experiments)"],"results":[{"metric":"Policy regret (convex case)","value":"O(√T)"},{"metric":"Local regret (nonconvex case)","value":"O(√((1+V)T)), where V measures system variation"},{"metric":"Adaptation speed","value":"GAPS adapts faster than follow-the-leader-type baseline in MPC with imperfect disturbance predictions (qualitative)"},{"metric":"Performance vs. optimal control baseline","value":"GAPS outperforms strong optimal control baseline in nonlinear system with non-i.i.d. disturbances (qualitative)"}],"novelty_claims":["First algorithm (GAPS) to achieve optimal policy regret (O(√T)) for online policy selection under convexity, closing the previous log(T) gap.","First local regret bound for online policy selection when convexity does not hold.","GAPS requires only partial derivatives along the visited trajectory and is computationally efficient (O(log T) matrix multiplications per step), unlike prior methods requiring oracle access and expensive resimulation.","Generalizes previous results by introducing a contractive perturbation property that includes disturbance-action controllers and other policy classes as special cases.","Bridges online policy selection and online optimization by transferring regret guarantees from online gradient descent to policy selection under the contractive property."]}

methods: Gradient-based Adaptive Policy Selection (GAPS) algorithm
General analytical framework for online policy selection via online optimization
Contractive perturbation property for policy-induced closed-loop dynamics
problems: Online adaptive policy selection in time-varying discrete-time dynamical systems
Minimizing total stage costs in systems with time-varying costs and dynamics
Achieving optimal regret bounds for online policy selection, both in convex and nonconvex settings

results.value: O(√T)
O(√((1+V)T)), where V measures system variation
GAPS adapts faster than follow-the-leader-type baseline in MPC with imperfect disturbance predictions (qualitative)
GAPS outperforms strong optimal control baseline in nonlinear system with non-i.i.d. disturbances (qualitative)

novelty_claims: First algorithm (GAPS) to achieve optimal policy regret (O(√T)) for online policy selection under convexity, closing the previous log(T) gap.
First local regret bound for online policy selection when convexity does not hold.

GAPS requires only partial derivatives along the visited trajectory and is computationally efficient (O(log T) matrix multiplications per step), unlike prior methods requiring oracle access and expensive resimulation.
Generalizes previous results by introducing a contractive perturbation property that includes disturbance-action controllers and other policy classes as special cases.
Bridges online policy selection and online optimization by transferring regret guarantees from online gradient descent to policy selection under the contractive property.

novelty_claims: Proposes a feature enhancement module (FEM) that uses cross attention to suppress inter-class similarity noise and enhance intra-class relevance in feature correlations.
Introduces a correlation reconstruction module (CRM) that encodes extra correspondence relations between foreground and background and incorporates multi-scale context semantic features.
Keeps background information in support features to generate dense integral correlations, improving correspondence learning.
Employs a multi-scale guidance module to fuse global context semantic features, capturing more diverse and complex context information.
Achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard few-shot segmentation benchmarks.

novelty_claims: Proposes a global-local feature enhanced network (GLF-Net) specifically designed for UAV-based object detection.
Introduces a backbone combining involution and self-attention for effective feature extraction from complex UAV images.
Develops a multiscale feature fusion module to improve detection of small and dense objects.
Designs a rotated regional proposal network based on midpoint offset representation for accurate detection of rotated objects.
Achieves state-of-the-art detection accuracy and generalization on both public and newly created UAV datasets.
